<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">In his</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://make.wordpress.org/meta/2015/03/04/new-plugin-directory-theme/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>announcement</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">about updates to the plugin directory, WordPress.org contributor Scott Reilly said that a backend reimplementation of the directory is on the roadmap for a future update. If you find a bug in the current implementation, feel free to open a ticket on</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://meta.trac.wordpress.org/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>meta.trac</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Wed, 04 Mar 2015 09:25:16 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Sarah Gooding\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:27;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:27:\"Matt: WordPress iOS WYSIWYG\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:21:\"http://ma.tt/?p=44747\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:43:\"http://ma.tt/2015/03/wordpress-ios-wysiwyg/\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:499:\"</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">It’s been a long road, but the WordPress mobile apps are finally making some major strides.</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://apps.wordpress.org/2015/02/26/wordpress-for-ios-a-new-visual-editor-more/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>WordPress iOS version 4.8 includes a visual editor so you won’t see code anymore when blogging on the go</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. (For anyone curious at home, WordPress originally</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/news/2005/12/wp2/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>shipped with WYSIWYG in version 2.0</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, and it was highly controversial at the time.)</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Wed, 04 Mar 2015 04:31:00 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:4:\"Matt\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:28;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:36:\"Akismet: February 2015 Stats Roundup\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"http://blog.akismet.com/?p=1789\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:63:\"http://blog.akismet.com/2015/03/04/february-2015-stats-roundup/\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:3152:\"</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">February was</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://blog.akismet.com/2015/02/01/january-2015-stats-roundup/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>another</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">slow spam month for Akismet. We didn’t even hit 200 million comments on any day this month. You can see the daily breakdown of the spam and ham comments Akismet caught in the graph below:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The busiest day was the first of the month, with about 178 million spam comments — and the slowest day was the 18th with about 106 million.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.jpeg)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>The total number of spam messages caught this month was 4,090,182,500</b></span><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. To visualize this, let’s say each spam is represented by</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://prezi.com/roben6fpzshd/blades-of-grass-in-a-football-field/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>one blade of grass in a football field</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">— to commemorate the football season ending this February.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">How many football fields would it take to cover that much spam? Twelve and a half.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">How about the real comments? We got a total of 131,465,000 of those this month. And if each one were represented by a blade of grass, they would take just under one half of a football field to account for. As always, real comments account for much much less than spam comments — about 3% this month.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">This month was unusually low in spam numbers not only compared with last month (with a 14% decrease in volume), but also since last year — decreasing by 38% compared with February 2014.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">We missed only about 1 in every 10,917 spam comments this month — not bad!</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Your own blog’s stats may have followed a similar pattern of decreased spam activity this month. If you are ever finding that the spammers are winning and more comments than usual are getting through Akismet’s filters, please feel free to reach out and let us know. We’re happy to look into it and help restore order :)</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Oblique;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><i>This post is part of a monthly series summarizing some stats and figures from the Akismet universe. Feel free to browse</i></span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://blog.akismet.com/category/monthly-roundup/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier-Oblique;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><i><u>all of the posts in the series</u></i></a><span style="font-family:Courier-Oblique;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><i>.</i></span>

  
 **(unknown attachment: dyn.age8u)** 
 
 **(unknown attachment: com.compuserve.gif)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Wed, 04 Mar 2015 01:03:53 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:7:\"Valerie\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:29;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:83:\"WPTavern: Jason Schuller’s Pickle Theme Re-Imagines WordPress as an Invisible CMS\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39892\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:90:\"http://wptavern.com/jason-schullers-pickle-theme-re-imagines-wordpress-as-an-invisible-cms\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:10856:\"</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.jpeg)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Last week Jason Schuller launched his Pickle WordPress theme on</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://pickle.pub/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Pickle.pub</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">and the product is now available on</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://creativemarket.com/circa75/208774-The-Pickle-Theme-for-WordPress%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Creative Market</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. Pickle is a restaurant theme that is packaged with a custom admin design to provide a seamless content-editing experience.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Schuller’s decision to</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/jason-schuller-to-re-enter-wordpress-theme-market-with-niche-admin-designs%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>re-enter the WordPress theme market</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">following the sale of Press75 came after several years of experimenting with alternative publishing platforms. In an</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/interview-with-jason-schuller-founder-of-press75-com%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>interview</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">with the Tavern last year, he expressed dissatisfaction with trying to make WordPress do what he wanted, which caused him to consider abandoning the platform entirely.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Schuller found himself chronically at odds with WordPress’ limitations for scaling its complexity backwards to provide a more simplified publishing experience. Pickle was born out of this frustration. The theme reimagines the WordPress admin as an extension of the front-end design, with no abrupt transitions for editing content.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.jpeg)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>Targeting a Wider Market Beyond WordPress</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">At first glance, it might appear that the restaurant niche is a relatively small and limited market for a WordPress theme developer. However, if you check out</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://pickle.pub/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Pickle.pub</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, you’ll find no mention of WordPress among Pickle’s features. Schuller is intentionally marketing it to a larger potential customer base that includes anyone looking to build a simple restaurant website.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“I’m not really advertising Pickle as a WordPress theme,” Schuller told the Tavern. “Essentially, my approach was to use WordPress to create my own custom CMS for minimalist restaurant websites.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">In the future he plans to release more options, styles and add-ons for the product. Currently, all of Pickle’s functionality is packed into the theme, but Schuller is not overly concerned about data portability in this instance.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“That data (in my opinion) is exclusive to what I’m doing with Pickle,” he said. “In other words, I’m not concerned with my users even knowing that it’s powered by WordPress.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Schuller is hoping to attract two different markets: customers who know they want WordPress and those who just know they want a business website and don’t care what software it uses.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“It shouldn’t matter to new users if it’s a WordPress solution,” he said. “But at the same time, freelancers who work with WordPress and have clients in the restaurant industry might be attracted by Pickle because it is a WordPress solution. I’m hoping to target both ends of the spectrum.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">This time around in the WordPress theme business, Schuller is venturing into the frontier where customers aren’t already convinced of a favorite CMS. Pickle was intentionally designed to make WordPress, and all its complexity, effectively invisible. This is one of the reasons the theme does not currently support the use of 3rd-party plugins.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>At Odds with WordPress Theme Development Best Practices</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">WordPress core doesn’t make it easy for developers to heavily customize the admin. This will soon change when the WP REST API lands in core; Schuller is open to updating Pickle to use the API once it’s no longer under heavy development.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“Once the REST API lands in core, there would be no reason for me not to change my approach,” he said. “But for today, a little custom CSS and PHP will do just fine.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Pickle is Schuller’s attempt at testing the waters for the possibility of other niche admin designs in the future. A hosted version is also set to launch within the next month. “If all goes well, I’ll probably create more niche solutions from the simple HTML templates I’ve been releasing on</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://leeflets.com%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Leeflets</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">,” he said. These include other one-page designs for things like newsletters, biographies, galleries, and landing or product pages.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“If that’s something I do end up doing, I would probably create some sort of admin theming plugin in order to eliminate duplicating the work each time,” Schuller said. “I could see the result of that being its own product as well for WordPress.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">At the moment, he is not prioritizing putting the functionality into a plugin. However, the way Pickle is built is at odds with WordPress theme development best practices of separating plugin functionality from the theme’s design.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">If a major release of WordPress causes a break in Pickle, it’s not in a plugin where one could easily disable the functionality. A breaking change could possibly effect the site’s frontend design, without an update to Pickle. If the product were packaged as a theme plus plugin combination, users would be in a better position for updates from both core and Pickle.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Schuller contends that WordPress theme developers should have the option to add features in a more modular fashion:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">I realize that my approach for Pickle specifically probably isn’t the way most “WordPress” developers would have done it. The important thing is that I finished it, and the idea is out there regardless of how it was engineered.</span> 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">I’ve always felt that a good CMS should reflect the functionality you need for any given project. For instance, we shouldn’t assume that all themes should support links, comments, widgets, etc., or even posts for that matter. Some themes/users might only need “pages” which means that most of the admin menus in WordPress could and maybe should be hidden in that case. We have to manually add “theme support” within theme functions for features like “post thumbnails”, so why isn’t that the case for everything else?</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">After years of frustration with “the WordPress way,” Schuller is going his own way this time around. He finds himself at friction with WordPress best practices and the ability to serve a larger market of people who don’t care if a site is built on WordPress.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“To be honest, I really wasn’t concerned about what the WordPress developer community would think about how I engineered Pickle,” he said. “I know there are probably so many ways I could have done it better, or hired someone else to do it better for me.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">In creating Pickle, Schuller consciously chose to ignore his fear of the doing_it_wrong() brigade in order to deliver a product that he believes will be simple for customers to use.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">When I got started with WordPress back in 2007, I had no clue what I was doing, but I was creating things and putting them out there as I learned, which is how I grew Press75. Somewhere along the way, I became much too concerned with how the WordPress community might perceive what I was making and that’s when my business started to decline.</span> 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Instead of just being happy making things I was passionate about, I became obsessed with perfection and making sure everyone was going be happy with what I made. I’m not going to make that same mistake again. It’s so much more important to put your work out there (even if it’s not perfect) than to never put it out there at all in fear that someone might not agree with the way you did it.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Could business be as simple as building products that make both you and your customer happy? Do all WordPress sites need a long-term plan for data portability and separation of theme and plugin functionality?</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>Re-Imagining WordPress as an Invisible CMS</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The invisibility of the traditional admin in the Pickle theme is a tribute to WordPress’ flexibility as a CMS. However, the lack of theme/plugin functionality separation is my primary objection to how it’s built, as it may make it difficult for the user to keep pace with core updates. This could potentially become a security issue.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Schuller’s approach for one-page designs may not conform to best practices but it once again begs the question: how can we erase the separation between editing experience and the display of content? Many users find the native customizer in its current state to be too clunky to adequately handle this in an elegant way.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">While I don’t fully support the approach that Schuller took with building Pickle, I agree with the basic premise of pushing the boundaries to simplify WordPress for the user. Pickle is inspirational, despite its technical drawbacks. It is a groundbreaking example of a WordPress-powered content editing experience that is perfectly tailored to the frontend design. It’s a design-specific theme that doesn’t require a heavy page builder or multiple sub-panels of customizer options.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Not everyone agrees on the best way to make the WordPress editing experience better while moving theme development forward. The platform needs people who are dissatisfied with the status quo to spearhead new, unorthodox ways of solving problems. However, it also needs the folks who have managed to keep inspiration alive for years, while working on the less glamorous tasks of contributing to core and establishing standards to make it better for everyone.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">As niche admin designs become common, the answer to the question of “What does WordPress look like?” will get fuzzy and difficult to define. A more modular approach to theming WordPress as a whole will make it easier for developers to sift out the functionality that users don’t need on basic websites. Finding a happy balance here will be critical for the platform to continue its reputation as a user-friendly CMS.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Tue, 03 Mar 2015 23:51:07 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Sarah Gooding\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:30;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:43:\"WPTavern: There Can Only Be One Sticky Post\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39916\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:53:\"http://wptavern.com/there-can-only-be-one-sticky-post\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:1817:\"</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">photo credit:</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://www.flickr.com/photos/37996585435@N01/6369443095%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Free Thinking at the Sage, Gateshead</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">–</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>(license)</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Last month, we</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/do-you-use-the-sticky-posts-feature-in-wordpress%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>polled our readers</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">to find out how many use the sticky posts feature in WordPress. Out of 322 votes, 66% said no while 34% said yes.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\nNote: There is a poll embedded within this post, please visit the site to participate in this post\'s poll.\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Use cases included, featuring content in a slider, portfolio sites, and using it on a directory site. For those who sticky one post at a time, I discovered a convenient plugin that removes the sticky flag from previous sticky posts.</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/plugins/there-can-be-only-one/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>There Can Be Only One,</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">developed by Alex Jones, ensures that only one sticky post is published at a time.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">There are no options to configure. Simply install, activate, and configure which post you want to sticky. When using this plugin, you no longer have to remove the sticky flag from the previous post which saves time and effort. There Can Only Be One is Jones’ first plugin submitted to the plugin directory and he’s interested to hear any feedback or suggestions you have to improve it.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Tue, 03 Mar 2015 22:04:09 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Jeff Chandler\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:31;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:69:\"WPTavern: WordCamp St. Louis, MO, March 14th-15 Will Have a Kids Camp\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39883\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:76:\"http://wptavern.com/wordcamp-st-louis-mo-march-14th-15-will-have-a-kids-camp\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:3758:\"</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>WordCamp St. Louis</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, MO, takes place on March 14th-15th on the</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/location/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>campus of Washington University</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. Tickets are</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/tickets/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>still available</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">for $25 which covers both days and gets you a T-Shirt, access to events, the after party, and community day.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The event features two days of learning about WordPress with</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/schedule/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>four tracks of sessions</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. Two tracks are dedicated to developers while the other two are devoted to users. In a stroke of luck, the first day of WordCamp takes place on</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://www.piday.org/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Pi Day,</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">which means</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://pi-pizza.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>free Pi pizza</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">for attendees.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">After learning about WordPress on Saturday, folks can immediately apply their knowledge and help others on Sunday which is</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/2015/01/26/community-day/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Community Day</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. Community day is similar to a WordPress contributor day in that there will be plenty of opportunities to learn, network, and share information. The schedule for community day looks like this:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>Keynote</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">by a well-know member of the WP community</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>Kids Camp</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">a class geared towards 8-13 year olds, however, any age is welcome to attend (space permitting). Parents must be present for children under 13 and all attendees must bring a laptop. Up to 3 children under the age of 18 can attend without charge for each paid adult (parent) ticket.</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>Hackathon</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">help developers, designers, and other attendees on creating or improving local organization’s presence on the web (the list of specific organizations will be announced later, if you know of someone who might benefit</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/contact/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>drop us a line</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">)</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>All-day happiness bar:</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">come and learn with the best!</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>Give back to Core:</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">members of the plugin and theme review team as well as core contributors will be on hand to help you give back to the global WordPress community.</span>
- <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://stlouis.wordcamp.org/2015/session/kids-camp/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#00eeff;"><u>Kids Camp</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">takes place on community day from 9am to noon and will cover the basics of setting up a site and what it means to publish to the web. The class is geared towards 8-13 year olds, but any age is welcome to attend. Parents or legal guardians must be present for children under 12 and all attendees must bring a laptop. Also on Sunday,</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://halfelf.org/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Mika Epstein</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">will give a keynote presentation that explains how to fit into the WordPress community by being true to yourself.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">I will be in attendance so if you see me, please stop and say hi as I’d love to chat with you. If you’re going to WordCamp St. Louis, let me know in the comments.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Tue, 03 Mar 2015 08:29:25 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Jeff Chandler\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:32;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:86:\"WPTavern: ThemeConf: A New Conference for Designers, Themers, and Front-End Developers\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39807\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:93:\"http://wptavern.com/themeconf-a-new-conference-for-designers-themers-and-front-end-developers\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:3571:\"</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.jpeg)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Designers and front-end developers who work with open source software have an exciting new event to look forward to in the UK this summer.</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://themeconf.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>ThemeConf</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">was announced today – a brand new conference created to help themers, designers, and front-end developers recharge their batteries with a variety of unique sessions on the world of themes.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The event will be hosted in Keswick, England, June 18-19th at the Alhambra Cinema, one of Britain’s oldest cinemas.</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://automattic.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Automattic</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">is ThemeConf’s first sponsor and three of the company’s employees are organizers for the event, including</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://twitter.com/karmatosed%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Tammie Lister</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">,</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://twitter.com/jacklenox%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Jack Lenox</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, and</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://twitter.com/schwuk%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>David Murphy</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“Jack always had the idea for a conference like this; he also wanted something in his local area,” Lister said. “I also wanted a conference that is theme focused. We all started talking over a Christmas meetup and this is what happened from that.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>Exploring the Future of Theming Beyond Specific Platforms</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The conference will focus on theming for open source applications such as WordPress, Drupal, Ghost, and Joomla. Themers of all experience levels are welcome, as are front-end developers who are interested in theming. The topics for the first year will be focused around the future of theming.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">“We hope to bring a wide range of talks under that banner,” Lister said. “We are currently curating our schedule, but it’s shaping up to be really interesting and diverse. We are planning on making it a place themers can go to get inspired.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">In selecting the sessions, organizers hope to further delve into the technologies and styles, beyond specific platforms, to explore the future of theming. They also plan to incorporate talks on performance and user experience.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Jack Lenox wants to focus the event around some of the most recent and significant shifts in theming and front-end development. “These go beyond the platform,” he said. “From REST APIs, to new design directions and into the burgeoning ecosystem of the new JavaScript tools such as React – tools that allow us to build engaging, human user experiences alongside a REST API.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">As one platform cannot possibly hold a monopoly on technical innovation, it’s important for designers and developers to venture outside the bubble of their primary platform in order to stay on the cutting edge. ThemeConf organizers are aiming to facilitate cross-platform collaboration and knowledge sharing to help attendees explore a wide range of topics.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The event will be non-profit, with any proceeds going into the next year and/or donated to the local area. The 101-year-old Alhambra Cinema is an intimate venue that will be limited to 150 attendees. If you’re interested in exploring the future of theming with other fans of open source in Keswick, make sure to</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://themeconf.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>sign up for the ThemeConf newsletter</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Mon, 02 Mar 2015 23:13:36 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Sarah Gooding\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:33;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:96:\"WPTavern: It’s Time For WordPress to Automatically Update Themes, Plugins, and Core by Default\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39846\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:101:\"http://wptavern.com/its-time-for-wordpress-to-automatically-update-themes-plugins-and-core-by-default\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:5182:\"</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Over the weekend, the</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/plugins/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>WordPress plugin directory</u></a> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://poststatus.com/popular-wordpress-plugins/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>implemented a major change</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">that better reflects how popular a plugin is. The number of total downloads has been replaced with the number of active installs. While the numbers are not exact, they’re close enough to give people insight into usage.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Active Sites\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">When it comes to reporting WordPress plugin security vulnerabilities, this is a welcome change. The active install numbers give the media a better idea on how many sites are potentially at risk. In addition to knowing the active installs for a given plugin, we can also see a breakdown of which versions are used.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:15.428571701049805pt;"><b>Using Outdated Versions of Plugins</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">With</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/plugins/jetpack/stats/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Jetpack,</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">we see that nearly 40% of sites use the latest version while the other 60% use an older version.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Active Plugin Versions\n</span>
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/plugins/wordpress-seo/stats/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#00eeff;"><u>Yoast SEO</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">is split down the middle with 50% of sites using the latest version and the other combined 50% using an older version.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">YoastSEO Plugin Version Breakdbown\n</span>
 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Only a quarter of the sites using</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/plugins/contact-form-7/stats/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Contact Form 7</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">are using the most recent version, while nearly 75% combined are using an outdated version.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">For other plugins, the trend is the same. A majority of sites are using older versions of plugins. For all of the effort that goes into informing users to keep sites up to date as a bare essential security practice, these are sobering statistics.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:15.428571701049805pt;"><b>Time to Auto Update All The Things</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">While I realize there isn’t always an immediate need to update a plugin</span> <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>unless it’s a security release</b></span><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, it’s a good idea to keep them updated regardless. These statistics indicate that the only way to keep as many sites as possible updated is to forcefully turn on automatic updates for major releases, minor releases, plugins, and themes.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">A move like this would likely generate a lot of push back, especially from those</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/how-will-you-configure-auto-updates-in-wordpress-3-7%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>who already don’t like</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">WordPress’ current implementation since there’s not an option in the WordPress backend to configure them. However, the numbers indicate that millions of sites are running outdated code by choice which is unacceptable.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">photo credit:</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://www.flickr.com/photos/8399025@N07/2146968879%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Rescue device</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">–</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>(license)</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The WordPress development team is in a position to help make the web safer for everyone. If all it takes to get people to support automatic updates for themes, plugins and core, is to add some UI to the WordPress backend to configure them, then so be it. I think it’s a small price to pay to quickly improve the situation and move in the right direction.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">While I don’t like having my hand held by software to keep things up to date, it appears as though there is no other choice. Automatic updates to minor releases is a good first step, but it needs to auto update everything because leaving the responsibility up to users is</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/wordpress-version-stats-updated-more-than-13-of-sites-are-running-wordpress-4-1%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>clearly not working</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Mon, 02 Mar 2015 22:24:38 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Jeff Chandler\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:34;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:78:\"WPTavern: Chris Wiegman, Former Lead Developer of iThemes Security, Joins 10up\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39387\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:86:\"http://wptavern.com/chris-wiegman-former-lead-developer-of-ithemes-security-joins-10up\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:3795:\"</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.png)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Chris Wiegman, the creator of</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wordpress.org/plugins/better-wp-security/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Better WP Security</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">, has</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://www.chriswiegman.com/2015/02/moving-on-to-new-endeavors/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>announced</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">that he’s moved on from iThemes to join</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://10up.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>10up</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. In late 2013,</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://wptavern.com/ithemes-acquires-better-wp-security-plugin-and-hires-wordpress-security-expert-chris-wiegman%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>iThemes acquired Better WP Security</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">and changed its name to iThemes Security. As part of the deal, Wiegman was brought on board to continue developing the plugin.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:15.428571701049805pt;"><b>Wiegman Moves From Products Back to Client Work</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Joining</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://10up.com/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>10up</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">gives Wiegman the opportunity to take what he’s learned from developing iThemes Security and apply it to client sites. He notes two huge reasons for the move: burnout and losing focus on</span> <span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;"><b>why</b></span> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">he was developing the product.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">To put it simply I was bored and burned out with the plugin. If I’m really going to be honest this was the case when I sold it to iThemes in 2013 and at the time I truly believed that working on it full-time would somehow renew my interest but that wasn’t the case. This is not to say I didn’t have a passion for it but just that I really wanted to work on other things and not just the plugin.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">This is an interesting move, because generally I see a lot of WordPress consultants branch away from client work to focus on products. In this case, a product driven developer has decided to go back to client work for the variety and educational opportunities it offers.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:15.428571701049805pt;"><b>The Future of iThemes Security</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Since its creation, Wiegman has been the face of Better WP Security and iThemes Security. iThemes CEO, Cory Miller, has this to say about Wiegman’s work.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">We thank Chris Wiegman for his contribution to iThemes Security (formerly known as Better WP Security) and his time at iThemes. We hope to make him proud of leaving such an awesome project in our hands. We are committed to making iThemes Security the easiest tool to allow people to protect and secure their WordPress websites.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">He also explains that while Wiegman is no longer with the company, iThemes will continue to develop the plugin, “One benefit of iThemes having been around for seven years now, with a 20-plus person team, is having ‘bench strength’ or rather, people who are able and available to take over projects such as iThemes Security in the event someone moves on to another opportunity.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://profiles.wordpress.org/chrisbliss18/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#00eeff;"><u>Chris Jean</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">along with</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://profiles.wordpress.org/aaroncampbell%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Aaron Campbell</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">will take over development of iThemes Security. Campbell will work with Jean to create a strong roadmap and assist in its future development. “The present and the future is bright for iThemes Security,” Miller said.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Mon, 02 Mar 2015 20:10:28 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Jeff Chandler\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:35;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:91:\"WPTavern: WordPress Version Stats Updated: More Than 1/3 of Sites are Running WordPress 4.1\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:28:\"http://wptavern.com/?p=39800\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:99:\"http://wptavern.com/wordpress-version-stats-updated-more-than-13-of-sites-are-running-wordpress-4-1\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:5964:\"</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Over the weekend, WordPress lead developer Andrew Nacin</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://make.wordpress.org/meta/2015/03/01/major-update-to-our-version-stats-for-php-mysql-and-wordpress/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>announced</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">a major update to WordPress version stats for PHP, MySQL, and WordPress. While the</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://wordpress.org/about/stats/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>stats page</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">looks much the same as before, more sophisticated methods of collecting and processing the data have been put in place, thanks to the efforts of WordPress.org contributor</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://profiles.wordpress.org/dd32%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>Dion Hulse</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">. The results are a dramatic shift in the data reported.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: public.jpeg)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>More Than 1/3 of Sites Running on WordPress 4.1</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">One of the most notable differences is the percentage of sites that are running the latest version of WordPress. Things are not quite as grim as previous stats indicated. Prior to the update, WordPress was reporting fewer than 10% of sites running on 4.1. After eliminating data for sites that are no longer online, the new data shows roughly 36% of sites running on the latest version.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Andrew Nacin explained how the new stats are collected and interpreted into the updated pie chart:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Thousands of new WordPress sites come online every day. Some others, though, stop pinging over time. The new data only reflects sites that have pinged api.wordpress.org within the last few months. There were also plenty of other inconsistencies in the data that we’ve been able to resolve, which has resulted in numbers we feel are more consistent and accurate.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Other major shifts in the data demonstrate that there are very few sites still lingering on WordPress 3.0, which was released in June 2010. Sites running on 3.0 were previously reported as 16.06% but are now ringing in at just 2.27%.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The same goes for PHP 5.2 usage, which was previously reported at an alarming 31.76%. More accurate data puts this figure at 16.60%. This is likely due to eliminating sites that haven’t pinged api.wordpress.org within the last few months.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">While the PHP version usage outlook is no longer as bleak as previous data indicated, Nacin said it isn’t enough to change the minimum required for WordPress core:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">One-sixth of all sites running PHP 5.2 is still many millions of sites. If we move the PHP minimum version too early, we risk stranding millions of installs on older versions of WordPress.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">So, I wish to note that this does not change our calculations for keeping PHP 5.2 as the minimum for WordPress core — we had these numbers available to us when preparing our 2015 plans.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The new stats are a welcome update to many involved in supporting and updating WordPress sites, but several commenters requested a more detailed cross of PHP version as compared to the WordPress version that sites are using.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://twitter.com/miss_jwo%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#00eeff;"><u>@miss_jwo</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">I\'ve been whining about seeing a cross for literally years… :)</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">— Andrey Savchenko (@Rarst)</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://twitter.com/Rarst/status/572400268467408896%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>March 2, 2015</u></a>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>

<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">WordPress developer Josh Pollock</span> <a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22https://make.wordpress.org/meta/2015/03/01/major-update-to-our-version-stats-for-php-mysql-and-wordpress/#comment-7656%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#e4afaff;"><u>commented</u></a> <span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">on the announcement post with the same question: “Is there anyway we can see PHP/MySQL versions broken down by what WordPress version they are running on? It would be interesting to see what percentage of users running an EOLed version of PHP are choosing to stay on old WordPress versions of their own accord/lack of action.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">I checked with Andrew Nacin, who confirmed that this data is available and will be made public at some point in the future. “We’re still working on ensuring the numbers are stable,” he said. “They’re pretty predictable: older WP versions have more people on older PHP and MySQL versions. Newer WP versions have less.”  There’s currently no set timeline for delivering the additional stats, but WordPress.org contributors have more data in the works.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier-Bold;font-size:12pt;"><b>WordPress is Urging Hosting Companies to Use More Recent Versions of PHP</b></span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">The WordPress project continues to work with hosting companies to urge them to update their PHP version offering to  5.4+. This is a slow but necessary process before WordPress can raise the minimum required PHP version, which Nacin said is ultimately part of the plan:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">PHP 5.2 is at about 16% for all installs right now. It’s at about 10% for installs running WordPress 4.1, but because 4.1 is such a large part of the pie (36%), it’s the WP version with the most PHP 5.2 installs.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Our goals remain the same: priority 1 is to update old WordPress installs, priority 2 is to update old PHP and MySQL. Only once the numbers drastically move as a result of our efforts would any minimum requirement change. We cannot risk abandoning so many users on older WordPress and PHP versions.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Ensuring the accuracy of WordPress usage stats is a major milestone. At the moment, the number of users on PHP 5.2 is far too high to consider a minimum requirement change any time soon. However, Nacin said that hosts have so far been very responsive to WordPress’ urges to update PHP versions. “Right now we’re still primarily in a research phase, but hosts have been very receptive and proactive in this area.”</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:7:\"pubDate\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:31:\"Mon, 02 Mar 2015 19:31:26 +0000\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}s:32:\"http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/\";a:1:{s:7:\"creator\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"Sarah Gooding\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}}}}i:36;a:6:{s:4:\"data\";s:13:\"\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n	\n\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";s:5:\"child\";a:2:{s:0:\"\";a:5:{s:5:\"title\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:26:\"Joseph: WIRED on WordPress\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"guid\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:32:\"https://josephscott.org/?p=11657\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:4:\"link\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:60:\"https://josephscott.org/archives/2015/03/wired-on-wordpress/\";s:7:\"attribs\";a:0:{}s:8:\"xml_base\";s:0:\"\";s:17:\"xml_base_explicit\";b:0;s:8:\"xml_lang\";s:0:\"\";}}s:11:\"description\";a:1:{i:0;a:5:{s:4:\"data\";s:933:\"</span>
<a href="applewebdata://53B4E767-2D66-426A-B2AA-63A3781179F0/%22http://www.wired.com/2015/03/wired-dot-com-from-the-devs/%5C%22" rel="noopener" class="external-link" target="_blank" style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;color:#00eeff;"><u>WIRED.com has merged into one WordPress install</u></a><span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\nOur redesign is here thanks to a big under-the-radar project in March 2014, when we migrated 17 active WIRED blogs into a single WordPress install. If we did our jobs right, you barely noticed that happen.\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">No doubt that this was a big job.  Good to see it all come together, making their content easier to manage.</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">Unfortunately their new design includes the super annoying trend of covering up the text I was reading when I scroll up on the site:</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
 **(unknown attachment: dyn.age80s4pgpvsce)** 
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">\n</span>
<span style="font-family:Courier;font-size:10.285714149475098pt;">This makes the reading experience really painful.</span>
